data.title: Dow, LyondellBasell upgraded at Citi as energy shock reshapes chemicals outlook
data.description:
Rising geopolitical tensions in the Middle East and disruptions to global energy flows are reshaping the outlook for commodity chemicals, prompting analysts at Citi to upgrade several major U.S. producers.

In a March 12 research report, Citi analyst Patrick Cunningham raised ratings on Dow (DOW) and LyondellBasell (LYB) to Buy from a previous investment rating of Neutral, arguing that supply disruptions and higher oil prices could boost margins for North American chemical producers with access to low-cost natural gas feedstocks.

The report also reiterated Celanese (CE) as the firm’s top pick in the sector while lifting earnings estimates across several companies, including Westlake (WLK), Olin (OLN) and Eastman Chemical (EMN).

Middle East disruptions tighten supply and raise prices

The shift in outlook stems from a growing supply shock tied to the Iran conflict and disruptions affecting shipments and energy infrastructure across the Middle East.

According to the Citi report, the closure of key routes such as the Strait of Hormuz has pushed up energy prices and disrupted petrochemical production across Asia and Europe. That has forced shutdowns or reduced operating rates at a number of facilities, tightening supply across several chemical chains. 

Nearly 20% of global oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz, making the region critical to global energy markets. The resulting spike in oil and feedstock prices has increased costs for producers outside the United States, where natural gas prices have remained relatively stable due to strong domestic supply. 

The mismatch between oil and gas pricing is widening the cost advantage for North American producers that rely heavily on ethane feedstock derived from natural gas.

North American producers gain cost advantage

That feedstock advantage is expected to drive higher margins for U.S.-based chemical companies, particularly in the polyethylene and olefins markets.

Citi expects polyethylene prices to rise roughly 12 cents per pound in the first half of the year before gradually normalizing later in the cycle.

The analysts estimate the pricing increases could expand integrated margins by about 4 to 8 cents per pound for producers, particularly those with low-cost U.S. assets. 

At the same time, disruptions to methanol supply from the Middle East, where more than 15% of global capacity may be trapped by the conflict, could tighten markets for downstream chemicals such as acetyls and MTBE, further lifting spreads. 

Those dynamics led Citi to conclude that the market’s long-standing oversupply narrative for commodity chemicals may be shifting toward a tighter supply environment.

Citi raises earnings forecasts across the sector

Reflecting the improved outlook, Citi significantly raised its earnings estimates for several major chemical producers.

The firm now expects Dow’s (DOW) earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization to rise about 22% compared with prior estimates, while LyondellBasell’s (LYB) forecast climbs roughly 32% on stronger polyolefins margins and higher derivative spreads. 

Other revisions include a 12% increase in expected 2026 ebitda for Westlake (WLK), a 14% increase for Olin (OLN), and a 9% increase for Eastman Chemical (EMN), according to the report. 

Despite the improved outlook, Citi noted that the duration of the conflict remains a key uncertainty. Even if tensions ease quickly, the analysts believe logistics bottlenecks, supply disruptions and low inventories could keep chemical prices elevated for several quarters.

The firm’s base case assumes two to three quarters of market disruption before supply chains fully normalize.

In the longer term, the report suggests geopolitical risks could discourage future petrochemical investment in the Middle East, potentially increasing the strategic value of North American chemical assets.

data.title: PayPay stock pops up 19% in first trade after IPO
data.description:
PayPay (PAYP) stock opened at $19 per share in its market debut on Thursday following its $880M initial public offering.

Its shares priced at $16 per share on Wednesday, less than the $17-$19 range it had sought when marketing the offering.

Within minutes of the opening trade at ~12:46 PM ET, PayPay (PAYP) shares shaved the gain to 13%. It's still a decent showing in contrast with the broader equity markets that remain in the red. The S&P 500 fell 1.1% in early afternoon trading, and the Nasdaq lost 1.2%.

data.title: Ulta earnings preview: Traders bet market share and holiday demand will dominate the call
data.description:
Investors will be closely watching when Ulta Beauty (ULTA) reports its latest quarterly results after the market closes on Thursday, with attention focused not only on financial performance but also on management’s commentary during the earnings call. 

Prediction market traders are attempting to anticipate the themes executives are most likely to address. The highest-probability topics include market share and holiday demand, each carrying a 96% chance of being mentioned. Discussions around Space NK, a specialty beauty retailer, follow closely at 95%, while broader economic uncertainty is expected to surface with a 91% probability. 

Other potential talking points include luxury beauty trends and store traffic. Geopolitical developments in the Middle East may also come up. 

Outlined below are the probabilities forecasted towards what Ulta will say in its upcoming earnings report according to Kalshi traders 

  • Market Share — 96% chance.
  • Holiday — 96% chance. 
  • Space NK — 95% chance.
  • Uncertainty — 91% chance. 
  • Target — 86% chance.
  • Luxury — 75% chance. 
  • Middle East — 72% chance. 
  • Tariff — 71% chance.
  • Traffic — 67% chance. 
  • Store Opening — 60% chance. 
  • AI / Artificial Intelligence — 41% chance.
  • Reward — 34% chance.
  • Diamond — 27% chance. 
  • Wicked — 16% chance.

data.title: John Arnold flags refi risk for private credit software loans
data.description:
Former hedge fund manager John Arnold warned Thursday that a wave of maturing software loans is set to become a bigger problem for the current stressed private credit market.

In a social media note, Arnold said "private credit funds will be very reluctant to roll over software loans when they mature," particularly because many closed-end funds lack the flexibility to refinance debt while facing ongoing redemption pressure. 

A number of those loans were originally used to fund dividend payments to private-equity sponsors, Arnold wrote, leaving companies with limited cash to repay them at maturity. His comments come amid rising concern over software lending in private credit, with recent media reports that some lenders are reassessing software borrowers as AI disruption clouds corporate earnings durability.

Sponsors now face tough choices: inject fresh capital, raise new equity at potentially steep discounts, refi with higher-cost mezzanine debt, or walk away and accept losses, the co-chair of Arnold Ventures said.

"In every situation, the equity is worth less even if business performance hasn't changed," he added.

According to Apollo Global Management chief economist Torsten Slok, the software space is confronting roughly $40B in debt coming due in 2028, a large portion of which is concentrated among companies with lower B-rated credit profiles.

Private credit-tied ETFs: (VPC), (PRIV), (HYIN), (XLF), (KBWB), (KBWD), and (BIZD)

Software ETFs: (IGV), (IGPT), (XSW), and (AOTS).

data.title: China widens BHP iron ore ban amid contract talks - Reuters
data.description:
China has ​widened a ban on iron ore from BHP (BHP) for the second time in two weeks, ‌Reuters reported Thursday, escalating a protracted contract dispute with one of the largest supplier's of the key steelmaking ingredient.

State-run iron ore buyer China Mineral Resources Group told domestic steel mills and traders they would be prohibited ​from taking delivery of Newman fines, a popular BHP (BHP) iron ore stored at ports, starting late next week, according to the report.

China has gradually tightened restrictions ​on local steel mills and traders buying BHP (BHP) iron ore during the past six months as it ​negotiates the terms of the Australian company's 2026 supply contract; the government banned purchases of Jimblebar fines, another type of iron ore, in September, followed ‌by ⁠Jinbao iron ore in November.

Benchmark April iron ore prices (SCO:COM) on the Singapore ⁠Exchange ​rose by more than 4% to $108.95 in afternoon trading Thursday, ​reaching their highest since January.

data.title: Oil prices are likely to stay very high for an extended period, according to expert
data.description:
Olivier Blanchard, an economics professor, warns that oil prices are likely to stay very high for an extended period, well beyond current market levels. He attributes this to persistent risks in the Strait of Hormuz, where a 20 million barrel daily shortfall could drag on.

Blanchard sees very high oil prices persisting long-term, far above current levels. He bases this on the impossibility of fully protecting ships in the Strait of Hormuz from missiles or drones, given insufficient reaction time.

Iran has little incentive to stop threats, even if President Trump declares conflicts resolved, as ambiguity alone deters non-Iranian vessels, Blanchard implies. This risk keeps most shipping away, extending the 20 million barrel per day oil shortfall indefinitely.

Alternative supply boosts are minimal short-term, at most 2 million barrels daily from other sources. Strategic reserve releases, like 400 million barrels, add only 3-4 million barrels per day, says Blanchard.

Oil demand elasticity remains very low at -0.1 or less, limiting consumption drops despite price surges, he adds. Blanchard thus forecasts prices nearing $150-200 per barrel, or higher.

Energy ETFs: (XLE), (AMLP), (VDE), (XOP), (OIH), and (IXC)

Oil ETFs: (USO), (UCO), (DBO), (OILK), and (USL)

data.title: U.S. stocks sink as war against Iran intensifies, oil prices keep climbing
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages were lower on Thursday as oil prices continued to climb due to the escalating U.S.-Israel-Iran conflict.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last -1%, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -1.3%, and the Dow Jones (DJI) was -1%.

Over in the bond market, the benchmark 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was 2 basis points higher at 4.25%, while the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) rose 6 basis points to 3.72%.

Crude oil (CL1:COM) rose 9.3% to $95 per barrel on Thursday, while Brent (CO1:COM) was also higher at $99.

“I find it hard not to have a central scenario where oil prices will remain very high for a long time, higher than the market current prices,” said Olivier Blanchard, Robert Solow Professor of economics emeritus at MIT.

“There is no reason, whether or not Trump declares that war is over, to think that Iran will not continue for some time to threaten to destroy the ships that try. Why should they stop? The risk will thus remain sufficiently high that most non-Iranian ships will not take the risk. Thus, the shortfall of 20 million barrels a day is likely to last for long,” he added.

The conflict in the Middle East has triggered one of the largest supply shocks of global oil markets in history, leading to disruption of almost 7.5% of the worldwide supply, according to the International Energy Agency.

In economic news, the initial jobless claims barely budged in the past week. The U.S. trade deficit narrowed more than expected in January.

Also, housing starts unexpectedly rose in January, while building permits dropped more than consensus.

The Federal Reserve balance sheet will be released later in the day.

data.title: Stellantis in talks with Xiaomi and Xpeng to invest in European business -- Bloomberg
data.description:
Stellantis (STLA) is reportedly in talks with two Chinese auto manufacturers to invest in its struggling European operations by possibly acquiring a stake in one or more of its European brands.

According to sources cited by Bloomberg, executives have met with China’s Xiaomi (XIACF) (XIACY) and Xpeng (XPEV) to explore options for a possible investment in Maserati or one of Stellantis’ (STLA) other brands including Opel, Fiat, and Peugeot, in an effort to shore up the company’s European business.

By selling a stake to a Chinese entity, Stellantis (STLA) would gain access to advanced technology for electric vehicles and software – sidestepping U.S. restrictions on Chinese tech -- while also giving a Chinese automaker increased access to the profitable European market.

“As part of its normal course of business, Stellantis holds discussions with a range of industry players around the world on various topics, always with the ultimate aim of providing customers with the best mobility choices,” Stellantis said in a statement to Bloomberg. “The company does not comment on speculation.”

Bloomberg’s sources believe that the overhaul of its European business could lead to a further separation of its U.S. and European arms, though a complete breakup is not currently the focus of current discussions.

“Stellantis states in the most categoric terms that there is no truth in the suggestion that it is considering a plan to split the company,” the company added.

While the development pulled Stellantis shares off the record low, the stock remains in negative territory for nine consecutive days.

data.title: Dell says government contractors can't dictate how their tech is used
data.description:
Dell Technologies (DELL) CEO Michael Dell said that government contractors can't tell the government how to use their technologies.

“I don’t think a company can dictate to a sovereign government what it does with its tools,” Dell told Bloomberg TV on Thursday. “I just don’t think that's a workable model.”

Dell's comments were in response to a question about the Trump administration designating Anthropic (ANTHRO) a "supply chain risk" after it failed to reach an agreement with the Pentagon over how its AI technology would be used. As a result of the designation, government agencies and contractors will be required to phase out use of Anthropic's products.

Anthropic is suing to have the designation overturned.

data.title: Babcock & Wilcox falls amid short report from Wolfpack Research
data.description:
• Babcock & Wilcox (BW) dropped 12% amid a short report from Wolfpack Research. 
  • Wolfpack Research disclosed that it's short shares of Babcock & Wilcox (BW),
  • Babcock & Wilcox didn't immediately respond to Seeking Alpha's email request for comment.
